title: Air-fryer jacket potato
subtitle: Crisp skin, fluffy centre, faster than the oven.
excerpt: A large potato rubbed with oil and salt and cooked in the air fryer at 200°C for 45 to 50 minutes. The skin crisps as well as a conventional oven but in less time. Add butter and a filling of your choice. Serves one.
sourceNotes: Air-fryer jacket potato; the 200°C/45-minute timing is based on standard commercial air-fryer parameters for large baking potatoes.

recipe.batchNotes: Two potatoes fit most 4–5 litre air-fryer baskets. More than two and the skin won't crisp evenly.

content.text: A jacket potato in the air fryer takes about 50 minutes versus 70 to 80 in a conventional oven. The skin crisps at least as well, possibly better, because the hot circulating air hits all sides simultaneously. The result is a potato with a papery, salty crust and a dry, fluffy interior.

Use the biggest potato you can find: a 300–350 g baking potato fills the basket and gives a good proportion of skin to floury interior. Pierce it all over; a punctured potato won't burst, and the holes let steam escape so the interior stays dry rather than wet.
Pierce {{potato}} scrubbed potato all over with a fork, about 20 times. Rub the skin with {{rapeseed-oil}} of rapeseed oil and rub {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt all over the surface.

Preheat the air fryer to 200°C. Place the potato directly in the basket with space around it. Cook for 45 to 50 minutes until the skin is crisp and papery. Check for doneness by squeezing gently with an oven glove: it should give easily, or probe the centre with a skewer; it should slide in without resistance.
Leave the potato in the air fryer for 5 minutes with the machine off; it finishes in the residual heat. Cut a cross in the top, push in the sides to open it up, and add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter.

items.symptom: Skin is tough but interior is raw
items.cause: Potato too large, or temperature too high.
items.fix: For a 350 g+ potato, lower to 190°C and add 10 minutes. The skin tolerates heat well; the interior takes time.

items.symptom: Skin wrinkled and soft, not crispy
items.cause: Not enough oil, or potato was wet when oiled.
items.fix: Dry the skin thoroughly after scrubbing. Rub with oil generously and add the salt.

items.cause: Not pierced enough, or potato not pierced at all.
items.fix: Pierce all over with a fork. Without holes, steam is trapped inside and the interior goes gummy.

content.text: The jacket potato is British lunch at its plainest and most satisfying. Butter and salt is the classic. Baked beans and cheddar is the school-canteen standard. Tuna mayo, coleslaw, or a spoonful of leftover stew all work. The air fryer is the most reliable way to get a properly crisp skin when you don't want to heat a full oven for one potato.
